_Design for feature request #423: [Feature Request] Auto-fill job description from LinkedIn job URLs_

> [!WARNING]
> The `<!-- implementation-plan-json -->` block in the Implementation plan section is machine-parsed by the implement workflow. Edit the `- [ ]` checkboxes to adjust the plan — do not edit the JSON directly.

# Auto-fill job description from LinkedIn job URLs

## What the user wants

When the user pastes a LinkedIn job URL (e.g. `https://www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/4415502323`) into the "Import from URL" field in the Application modal, the job description should come through cleanly — with a structured metadata header (title, company, location, employment type) and the description body — instead of the navigation/sidebar noise that the generic HTML extractor currently produces. The output should match the visual quality of the existing Greenhouse, Lever, Eightfold, Workable, Workday, Uber, and Expedia handlers.

**No meaningful ambiguity.** The request is specific (LinkedIn, same UX as the other ATS handlers). One small product decision: which job-criteria rows from LinkedIn ("Seniority level", "Employment type", "Job function", "Industries") to include in the metadata header. Default chosen below: all four (mirrors how Workable and Eightfold include every populated structured field they have). Listed as an open question for owner override.

## Proposed implementation

Add a LinkedIn ATS handler that detects `www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/{numericId}` (and the equivalent `linkedin.com/jobs-guest/...` form) URLs, extracts the numeric job ID, and calls LinkedIn's **public unauthenticated** `https://www.linkedin.com/jobs-guest/jobs/api/jobPosting/{id}` endpoint — which returns a static HTML fragment (~60 KB, no JS rendering required) containing a structured "topcard" (title/company/location), a `description__job-criteria-list` block (employment type, etc.), and a `show-more-less-html__markup` container with the description body. Parse those four blocks with targeted regexes, build the metadata `<table>` header following the existing ATS pattern, and concatenate the description HTML. Fall through to the generic HTML scraper on any failure, matching the behavior of every other ATS handler.

### Files to modify or create

- `app/api/fetch-job-description/route.ts` — add `buildLinkedInMeta(parsed)` helper, `fetchLinkedInJob(jobId, signal)` async helper (calls the jobs-guest endpoint, parses the HTML fragment, returns `meta + body` string or `null`), and an ATS detection block inside `POST` that runs before the generic `fetch(parsed.toString())` call (alongside the other API-based handlers: Eightfold, Workable, Lever, Greenhouse). Pattern: detect URL → call helper → return on success, fall through on `null`.
- `__tests__/fixtures/linkedin-job.html` — committed real HTML snapshot of `https://www.linkedin.com/jobs-guest/jobs/api/jobPosting/4415502323` (the LinkedIn Principal Staff SWE posting referenced in the user request). Must be fetched and committed **before** the test file references it (per CLAUDE.md ATS rule).
- `__tests__/api/fetch-job-description.test.ts` — extend with a `describe('LinkedIn ATS (...)`)` block covering: happy path (real fixture → metadata + description), non-2xx API → falls through to HTML scraping, missing description block → falls through, non-matching URL → handler not triggered, URLs with `?trackingId=` and other query strings still match.
- `CLAUDE.md` — add LinkedIn row to the "ATS integrations already implemented" table and an "ATS-specific gotchas learned" bullet (notably: the public jobs view page has no JSON-LD server-side; the `jobs-guest/jobs/api/jobPosting/{id}` endpoint is the only reliable unauthenticated source, returns clean static HTML with named CSS classes — `topcard__title`, `topcard__org-name-link`, `topcard__flavor--bullet`, `description__job-criteria-list`, `show-more-less-html__markup`).
- `README.md` — add LinkedIn to the supported-ATS list wherever the others are mentioned (if such a list exists; otherwise no README change).

### UI changes

**No new UI.** The feature is complete without one — the existing "Import from URL" field in `components/modals/ApplicationModal.tsx` already calls `POST /api/fetch-job-description`. Adding LinkedIn detection inside that route automatically improves the result for any LinkedIn URL the user pastes; the modal's loading state, error toast, and rendered description block are unchanged. The user-visible difference is purely in the *content* the existing field returns: clean title/company/location/criteria header + description, instead of raw navigation/sidebar HTML.

- [ ] **Step 1: Capture LinkedIn fixture HTML** (~5 turns) — `curl` the real `jobs-guest/jobs/api/jobPosting/4415502323` endpoint into `__tests__/fixtures/linkedin-job.html` and verify the marker CSS classes are present.
- [ ] **Step 2: Add LinkedIn handler to fetch-job-description route** (~18 turns) — `buildLinkedInMeta` + `fetchLinkedInJob` helpers and a detection block in `POST` matching `www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/{numericId}`; falls through on any failure. Depends on Step 1 only because Step 3's tests need the fixture — Step 2's code does not, but in practice Step 1 should land first so the same commit can include passing tests.
- [ ] **Step 3: Add LinkedIn handler tests** (~16 turns) — full happy-path + 4 fallback-path tests in `__tests__/api/fetch-job-description.test.ts`, importing the fixture file. Depends on Steps 1 and 2.
- [ ] **Step 4: Update CLAUDE.md and README** (~6 turns) — add LinkedIn row to the ATS integrations table, add the gotchas bullet, and bundle into the same commit. Depends on Steps 2 and 3.

## Design decisions

**(a) `jobs-guest/jobs/api/jobPosting/{id}` over LinkedIn Voyager/GraphQL APIs.** The Voyager API requires authenticated session cookies and returns 401 server-side. The jobs-guest endpoint requires no auth, returns the same job content as a static HTML fragment, and is what LinkedIn itself serves to logged-out visitors who land on `/jobs/view/{id}` — so it is publicly stable. Verified: `curl` against this endpoint with the project's `USER_AGENT` returns HTTP 200, ~60 KB, with all five marker CSS classes (`topcard__title`, `topcard__org-name-link`, `topcard__flavor--bullet`, `description__job-criteria-list`, `show-more-less-html__markup`) populated.

**(b) Regex-parsing the HTML fragment over adding a real HTML parser (cheerio/jsdom).** Every existing ATS handler parses with targeted regexes; adding a new runtime dependency for one ATS would be inconsistent with the established codebase pattern and would add bundle weight to the route. The LinkedIn fragment is small (~60 KB, capped at MAX_BYTES = 500 KB by existing route logic) and uses stable named CSS classes — the same constraints under which regex parsing already works for Uber and Workday's JSON-LD blocks.

**(c) Match `/jobs/view/{numericId}` with a trailing-anchor `\d+` and ignore everything after it.** LinkedIn URLs in the wild include a SEO slug (e.g. `/jobs/view/principal-staff-software-engineer-...-at-linkedin-4415502323`), tracking params (`?trackingId=…&refId=…`), and `#hashes`. The numeric ID is the only authoritative key. Using `parsed.pathname.match(/^\/jobs\/view\/(?:[^/]+-)?(\d+)/?$/)` would also work; the simpler `^\/jobs\/view\/(\d+)/?$` would miss slugged URLs. Final choice: `^\/jobs\/view\/.*?(\d+)\/?$` against `parsed.pathname` — captures the trailing numeric ID regardless of slug, and `parsed.search`/`hash` are already discarded by `pathname`. (Alternative considered: stripping the slug client-side. Rejected: server-side detection is what the existing handlers do and keeps the modal logic unchanged.)

**(d) Fixture stored as a raw `.html` file rather than parsed JSON.** Every other ATS fixture is JSON because the upstream returns JSON. LinkedIn's jobs-guest endpoint returns HTML, so the fixture is HTML — storing parsed/derived data would be a layer of test-only abstraction that diverges from "the test data matches the real API response" (the explicit goal of the ATS-fixture rule in CLAUDE.md). Tests load the fixture with `readFileSync` from `node:fs` — no `?raw` Vite import suffix, no config dependency.

**(e) "Job function" and "Industries" criteria are included in the metadata table.** Both are real LinkedIn-populated fields and informative for job-tracking purposes (Job function ≈ "Department" row in other ATS handlers). Including them costs nothing on rendering. The alternative is filtering to only "Seniority level" + "Employment type"; rejected because the existing handlers (Workable, Eightfold) include every populated structured field they have. Owner can override — see Open questions.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] **User-visible**: User pastes `https://www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/4415502323` (or any other LinkedIn `/jobs/view/{id}` URL, including ones with slug prefixes and tracking query params) into the "Import from URL" field in the Application modal, clicks the import button, and the description field is populated with a clean `<h1>` title, a metadata `<table>` (company, location, seniority, employment type, job function, industries — whichever are populated), and the full job description body — visually consistent with how Greenhouse/Lever/Workday URLs already render.
- [ ] Pasting a LinkedIn URL with a slug in the path (e.g. `/jobs/view/principal-staff-software-engineer-development-infrastructure-at-linkedin-4415502323`) produces the same clean output as the bare numeric form.
- [ ] Pasting a non-job LinkedIn URL (e.g. `https://www.linkedin.com/feed/`) does NOT trigger the handler — the route falls through to the generic HTML scraper without an extra fetch to the jobs-guest API. (Verifiable by checking the network tab in DevTools or by inspecting the test for fetch call count = 1.)
- [ ] When the `jobs-guest` endpoint returns a non-2xx, the route silently falls through to the generic HTML scraper — the user sees the previous behavior (noisy but non-empty content), not a 502 error.
- [ ] `npm run test:coverage` passes locally with all coverage thresholds met (lines ≥85%, branches ≥80%, functions ≥65%).
- [ ] `npm run lint` and `npx tsc --noEmit` both pass.

## Open questions

1. **Which LinkedIn job-criteria rows to surface in the metadata header?** Default in this proposal: all four — Seniority level, Employment type, Job function, Industries. Other valid options: only Seniority + Employment type (matches Lever's row count), or only Employment type (matches Workday). Owner: confirm "include all four" or specify a subset before Step 2 starts.
